title: Wound Healing and Dressing Selection
subtitle: The nurse assesses a healing wound, distinguishes normal healing from infection, and selects the right dressing while changing it with aseptic technique to support moist wound healing.

patient.name: Wound Healing
patient.admitDate: The nurse assesses a healing wound, distinguishes healthy healing from infection, and selects and changes the appropriate dressing.

vitals.temp: Protein, vit C, zinc, glucose control

nursesNote: The nurse assesses a healing wound through the phases of healing: hemostasis, inflammatory (mild redness, warmth, and edema in the first 1-3 days is normal), proliferative (red granulation tissue and epithelialization), and maturation. A clean surgical incision with approximated edges heals by primary intention; an open wound left to granulate heals by secondary intention with more scarring. Drainage is described as serous (clear), serosanguineous (thin pink), sanguineous (bloody), or purulent (thick, colored, foul — a sign of infection). Healthy granulation is red and moist; yellow slough and black eschar are nonviable. The nurse keeps the wound bed moist (moist wound healing speeds epithelialization), selects a dressing matched to exudate, and changes it with aseptic technique. Increasing pain, spreading redness, purulent drainage, and fever signal infection and are reported. Adequate protein, vitamin C, zinc, perfusion, and glucose control support healing.
providerOrders: Assess wound color, drainage, edges, and peri-wound skin each change
Distinguish normal inflammation/granulation from signs of infection
Maintain moist wound healing and match the dressing to the exudate
Change dressings using aseptic technique; obtain cultures as ordered
Optimize protein, vitamin C, zinc, and glucose control; report infection

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about wound healing?

opts.text: Healing progresses through hemostasis, inflammatory, proliferative, and maturation phases
Mild redness, warmth, and swelling in the first few days is normal inflammation
Red, moist granulation tissue is a sign of healthy healing
Increasing pain, spreading redness, and purulent drainage suggest infection
A clean surgical incision with approximated edges heals by secondary intention
Black eschar is healthy tissue that should be left to heal
A wound healing by secondary intention is left open to granulate and scars more
Moist wound healing supports faster epithelialization than drying out
Serosanguineous drainage is thin and pink (serous plus blood)
Purulent drainage is a normal expected finding in all wounds
Adequate protein, vitamin C, and zinc support wound healing
Uncontrolled hyperglycemia and poor perfusion impair healing

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the steps of a dressing change

opts.text: Perform hand hygiene and gather supplies
Don clean gloves and remove the old dressing
Assess the wound and surrounding skin
Perform hand hygiene, don clean/sterile gloves, and clean the wound
Apply the new dressing and document the assessment
Red, moist granulation tissue in the wound bed
Thin serosanguineous drainage early after surgery
Thick green, foul-smelling drainage
New surrounding redness spreading outward with warmth
Edges approximating with new pink epithelial tissue
Increasing wound pain after day 3 with fever
Mild edema and warmth on postoperative day 1
Yellow slough and black eschar filling the wound

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Wound shows purulent drainage, spreading redness, and fever
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess the wound, drainage, and vital signs
Notify the provider of the suspected infection
Obtain a wound culture as ordered before antibiotics
Perform the ordered dressing change with aseptic technique
Document findings and monitor the response
Using aseptic technique during a dressing change
Letting a healing wound dry out completely
Choosing an absorbent dressing for a heavily draining wound
Leaving a saturated dressing in place for two days
Assessing wound color, drainage, and peri-wound skin each change
Ignoring spreading redness and a new fever
Optimizing protein intake and glucose control to aid healing
Reporting purulent, foul-smelling drainage to the provider

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Basic Care and Comfort, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
